Based on my web search results, I can provide you with a comprehensive overview of current research and trends related to your query about card scannability, eye-tracking, badge placement, and game UI design for 2024-2025:

## Eye-Tracking & F-Pattern Research

A 2025 eye-tracking experiment conducted by Jerry Lee of Wonsulting with recruiters reinforced the F-pattern and the importance of quantified achievements. Nielsen and Pernice (2010) famously identified the F-shaped reading pattern, where users prioritise top and left-aligned content. Fixation duration further distinguishes engagement from confusion—short fixations suggest scanning, while prolonged ones might indicate processing difficulty.

## Card Scannability & Metadata

Metadata should be brief (about 10 to 15 words, though it can be longer depending on the complexity of the information), supplementing the title with more in-depth context. Dense text gets skipped, aligning with general reading research — people skip over text blocks in favor of scannable content.

## Badge Placement

If the card has a preview, it's best to anchor the badge to the lower right side, overlaid on the preview. If there's no preview, you can place the badge in the footer on the left.

## Grid Systems & Dense Layouts

Use wider gutters (24–32 px) for spacious editorial layouts and narrower gutters (12–16 px) for dense data interfaces. Grid systems provide structural foundation while allowing creative flexibility for game-specific requirements. Proximity principles group related elements while separating unrelated functions.

## Game UI Trends 2024-2025

Overly complicated menus and crowded HUDs are a thing of the past. In 2025, the trend is toward clean, minimalist designs that focus only on what players need in the moment. This reduces cognitive load and helps players focus on gameplay. Adaptive UIs dynamically adjust their layout, content, and functionality based on the context of use, user preferences, and device capabilities. For example, a game UI might reconfigure itself when transitioning from desktop to mobile or when switching between different gameplay modes.

## HUD Design

Research in eye-tracking studies shows players follow predictable gaze patterns during gameplay—the F-pattern for menu reading and the center-focus pattern during action gameplay.
